course_description:
This course introduces research as a core practice skill that is important for generating, evaluating and using knowledge in social work and human services practice. Teaching modules concentrate on the social, political, cultural, and ethical context in which research knowledge is generated and used, key research principles and approaches, and skills in designing research and analysing qualitative and quantitative data.

Research has a central role in building knowledge for social work practice. It is important in generating new practice knowledge, challenging practice wisdom, informing policy debates, generating theory, testing new interventions, understanding consumer/client/citizen experiences of services and evaluating needs, programs, practices and policies. Research skills are thus an important part of developing a critical stance towards practice and engaging in knowledge generation and use. This course seeks to build core skills in social work research.

ASWEAS Required Curriculum Content Areas covered in this course:

The Bachelor of Social Work (Hons) program is accredited by the Australian Association of Social Workers. The Australian Social Work Education and Accreditation Standards (2020) stipulates that accredited social work programs are required to include content that addresses the five core curriculum areas listed below. This course contains content in the following curriculum area:

The BSocWk(Hons) will undergo re-accreditation in 2026 under the new ASWEAS (2024), which outlines updated required curriculum. This course content maps to the following:

Additional timetable information
This course is delivered via a blended learning approach.
The majority of the content are delivered as online modules available via Blackboard. These will be released approximately a week in advance of the workshops.
We will then have a weekly in-person workshop where we will apply the content learned via the online materials. In the first 6 weeks,
each week
there will be an in-class quiz worth 4% held during these workshops. In later weeks, the workshops will be the forum for your group Journal Club presentations. Part of this Journal Club task requires the facilitation of a group discussion. In the final weeks of the course, we will undertake activities to help you prepare for your final exam.
As such, it is essential that you attend the workshops and engage with the online materials ahead of the workshops.
course_aims:
The Australian Association of Social Workers Practice Standards (2023), Standard 5, requires social workers to be able to "practice within a professional knowledge framework informed by a critical understanding of contemporary social work theory and research." The course aims to develop core skills in using, critiquing, and planning research, to ensure students develop competencies and confidence as both informed users of published research and designers of small scale research projects in social work and human services contexts. This course will help develop your understanding of the logic underpinning research approaches, the methods that can be used to design, implement and analyse research, and the social, political, and ethical context in which research knowledge is generated and used.

For those planning on pursuing the honours stream of the BSocWk, this course will give you a solid foundation for undertaking your very own independent research project!

learning_outcomes.description: Describe the role of research and evaluation in informing ethical and effective social work practice within broader social, cultural, and policy contexts.
Explain the stages of the social work research process, from identifying an issue to disseminating findings, including the use of various research approaches.
Critically review and synthesise existing qualitative and quantitative social work research, identifying useful evidence, gaps and appropriate research questions.
Select and justify appropriate and ethical research designs and sampling techniques to address identified knowledge gaps in a social work context.
Select and adapt research dissemination strategies that are audience-appropriate and capable of influencing social work practice and policy.

assessment_details.title: Journal Club Presentations
assessment_details.task_description: This description is only a summary of the task. For full details, including the marking rubric, please refer to the course Learn UQ (Blackboard) site. Rationale The Australian Association of Social Workers (AASW) Practice Standards (2023) require social workers to “ practice within a professional knowledge framework informed by a critical understanding of contemporary social work theory and research ” (Standard 5) and to “ be active learners, open to new and emerging knowledge, research and evidence informing their practice ” (Standard 9). However, evidence suggests that social work practitioners report a lack of confidence in research knowledge and skills (DeFaia, 2015) and may dismiss the relevance of research to their practice (Cameron & Este, 2008). Journal clubs have been identified as a way of helping students and practitioners to become more confident in appraising research and to help show how research can inform practice (Moore et al., 2013). Using a journal club as an assessment task is informed by research on social work education that suggests that hands-on activities (Lei, 2008) and peer learning (Pan & Tang, 2005; Wilson, 2006, 2008) can reduce student anxiety and support learning about research. Task Description In your group (3–4 members), facilitate a 25 or 30-minute journal discussion with the class, based on your appraisal of research papers on a shared topic. For this task some elements of the task attract an individual mark and others related to group facilitation will receive a group mark. Please check the marking rubric on Blackboard. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extension requests are submitted online via my.UQ. Extension requests received in any other way will not be approved.
Extension guidelines
Extension requests should be submitted prior to the assessment deadline. Requests for extensions received after the assessment item submission due date may not be approved. Late requests must include evidence of the reasons for the late request as per the guidelines in my.UQ . The extension granted should be in proportion to the period of illness or disruption caused by the exceptional circumstances and will typically be no more than the specified maximum extension length for each assessment. A maximum of three assessment requests can be submitted for each assessment, and the third request must include an Assessment Management Plan. Students who have been ill or unable to attend class for more than 4 weeks in a semester are advised to carefully consider whether they are capable of successfully completing their courses and may need to consider applying for removal of course . Students who have a disability or a chronic medical condition (including a mental health condition) are encouraged to meet with a Disability Advisor within Student Services to request a Student Access Plan (SAP) with a clause on extension requests. Students who have an SAP can apply for a 7-day extension.
Outcome of application
Extension requests are generally processed within 2 working days.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .

assessment_details.title: End-of-Semester Exam
assessment_details.task_description: This description is only a summary of the task. For full details, including the marking rubric, please refer to the course Learn UQ (Blackboard) site. Rationale As you will learn in this course, a research proposal is a structured document that explains what you plan to research, why it’s important, and how you plan to investigate it. As a social worker, you may need to draft a research proposal in order to undertake practice-based research within your organisation, apply for grants/funding, or as part of future study or an academic career! Task Description In the exam, you will be provided with a brief AI generated literature review related to a particular social work research problem. You will be required to critique the AI generated literature review. You will then use the literature review as a stimulus to develop a research proposal that outlines the details of a research project that would help to address the identified knowledge gap. The exam will be a series of short answer questions that mirror the elements of a research proposal. Your responses must demonstrate engagement with and understanding of the SWSP3303 course material. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: Why does social work research matter? Welcome to SWSP3303! Please make sure you work through the week 1 online module on Blackboard before you attend this, our first workshop. In the workshop, we will begin by introducing the course and assessment. We will then do activities that build on and apply the material from the week 1 online module. We conclude the session with your first of six (1/6) in-class quizzes (10mins; 3-4 questions; 4% of total grade). It will assess your understanding of material from the required readings, online material and workshop for this week's content. Remember - only the best 5 scores out of the 6 quizzes will be counted towards your total mark for this assessment item. Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Week 2: Frameworks underpinning social work research Please make sure you work through the week 2 online module on Blackboard before you attend our workshop this week. This week, we will do activities that build on and apply the material from the week 2 online modules. For example, we will consider how different research paradigms can alter the way we approach research on the same topic. We conclude the session with your second of six (2/6) in-class quizzes (10mins; 3-4 questions; 4% of total grade). It will assess your understanding of material from the required readings, online material and workshop for this week's content. Remember - only the best 5 scores out of the 6 quizzes will be counted towards your total mark for this assessment item. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Week 3: How to conduct a literature review Please make sure you work through the week 3 online module on Blackboard before you attend our workshop this week. This week, we will do activities that build on and apply the material from the week 3 online modules. This will involve a library session on search strategies. We conclude the session with your second of six (3/6) in-class quizzes (10mins; 3-4 questions; 4% of total grade). It will assess your understanding of material from the required readings, online material and workshop for this week's content. Remember - only the best 5 scores out of the 6 quizzes will be counted towards your total mark for this assessment item. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Week 4: Developing your research question Please make sure you work through the week 4 online module on Blackboard before you attend our workshop this week. This week, we will do activities that build on and apply the material from the week 4 online module. These activities will help you identify the research gap and craft an answerable research question. We also introduce the concept and format of a 'Journal Club' to help get you ready for assessment task 2. We conclude the session with your fourth of six (4/6) in-class quizzes (10mins; 3-4 questions; 4% of total grade). It will assess your understanding of material from the required readings, online material and workshop for this week's content. Remember - only the best 5 scores out of the 6 quizzes will be counted towards your total mark for this assessment item. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Week 5: What to consider when designing your research Please make sure you work through the week 5 online module on Blackboard before you attend our workshop this week. This week, we will do activities that build on and apply the material from the week 5 online module. These activities will help further your understanding of different research designs. We continue to learn about Journal Clubs. We'll introduce you to the marking rubric for assessment 2 and you'll be asked to apply it to a 'not so good' example of a journal club. We'll then work together to identify how it could have been better. This will help you and your group with your preparations for your own Journal Club session! We conclude the session with your fifth of six (5/6) in-class quizzes (10mins; 3-4 questions; 4% of total grade). It will assess your understanding of material from the required readings, online material and workshop for this week's content. Remember - only the best 5 scores out of the 6 quizzes will be counted towards your total mark for this assessment item. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 6: Sampling - Where should you get your data from? Please make sure you work through the week 6 online module on Blackboard before you attend our workshop this week. This week, we will do activities that build on and apply the material from the week 6 online module. These activities will help further your understanding of different approaches to sampling. We continue to learn about Journal Clubs. Again, we'll use the marking rubric for assessment 2 to assess an example of a Journal Club session. We'll then work together to identify how it could have been better. This will help you and your group with your preparations for your own Journal Club session! We conclude the session with your last (6/6) in-class quiz (10mins; 3-4 questions; 4% of total grade). It will assess your understanding of material from the required readings, online material and workshop for this week's content. Remember - only the best 5 scores out of the 6 quizzes will be counted towards your total mark for this assessment item. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 7: Words Part 1 - How to collect qualitative data Please make sure you work through the week 7 online module on Blackboard before you attend our workshop this week. We are shifting up the formats of the workshops for the next few weeks as we are entering our Journal Club era! So, we'll begin the session with group Journal Club presentations (based on the previous week's content - sampling). Given that there are marks for facilitating group discussion, please come and support your peers, even if it is not your week to present. We'll then undertake some applied activities related to the content in this week's online learning materials about collecting qualitative data. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 8: Words Part 2 - How to make sense of qualitative data Please make sure you work through the week 8 online module on Blackboard before you attend our workshop this week. Again, we'll begin the session with group Journal Club presentations on qualitative data collection . We'll then undertake some applied activities related to the content in this week's online learning materials about analysing qualitative data. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 9: Numbers Part 1 - How to collect quantitative data Please make sure you work through the week 9 online module on Blackboard before you attend our workshop this week. Again, we'll begin the session with group Journal Club presentations. This time they'll be focused on qualitative data analysis . We'll then undertake some applied activities related to the content in this week's online learning materials about collecting quantitative data. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 10: Numbers Part 2 - How to make sense of quantitative data Please make sure you work through the week 10 online module on Blackboard before you attend our workshop this week. Again, we'll begin the session with group Journal Club presentations. This time they'll be focused on quantitative data collection . We'll then undertake some applied activities related to the content in this week's online learning materials about analysing quantitative data. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 11: How to share your findings with different audiences Please make sure you work through the week 11 online module on Blackboard before you attend our workshop this week. In this workshop we will look at examples of different research dissemination strategies and consider their audience and purpose. You'll then work in groups to develop a dissemination strategy for different stakeholders. Depending on the number of students enrolled in the course, we may also have another round of Journal Club presentations this week. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Week 12: Bringing it all together in a research proposal This week we bring everything you've learnt so far in the course together and talk about research proposals. There are a few resources listed on your Blackboard site. We'll look at more 'good' and 'not so good' examples of research proposals. We are doing this to help you to prepare for your final exam. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 13: Exam preparation There is no online material this week. Our workshop will be completely dedicated to exam preparation, so please join us! Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
